title: Facing Quarter-End Pressures: Understanding the Repo Market and Federal Reserve Tools

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This speech by SOMA Manager Roberto Perli explains the mechanics of repo market quarter-end pressures, clarifies that these pressures do not indicate reserve scarcity (reserves remain abundant), and details how the Standing Repo Facility (SRF) operates as a backstop only for the uncleared tri-party segment of repo markets. The speech outlines three distinct repo market segments (tri-party, interdealer, dealer-to-client) and explains why the SRF is not designed to be an effective ceiling for all repo rates, particularly dealer-to-client activity involving hedge funds.
key_points: Repo rates have moved higher relative to administered rates and EFFR over the past couple years due to growing Treasury securities financing demand, reduced Fed-supplied liquidity from balance sheet runoff, and intermediation frictions
Quarter-end pressures do NOT indicate reserve scarcity; reserve supply remains abundant according to the federal funds market elasticity measure and other indicators
Most recent September 2024 quarter-end: SOFR peaked roughly 21 basis points above its prior-week average, within the 10-25 bps range common in 2015-17 when reserves were clearly abundant

The repo market has three major segments: (1) tri-party repo (client-to-dealer, uncleared portion where SRF operates), (2) interdealer segment (cleared/netted via FICC), and (3) dealer-to-client segment (bilateral, mostly hedge fund financing)

The SRF is NOT designed to be an effective backstop for the dealer-to-client segment; borrowers there (primarily hedge funds) do not have direct SRF access, and SRF's afternoon settlement and lack of central clearing make it operationally costly for morning-settling activity

On one day at recent quarter-end, more than $500 billion of overnight repo against SRF-eligible collateral traded above the SRF rate, concentrated in interdealer and dealer-to-client segments where SRF is not an effective substitute
why_it_matters: Quarter-end repo rate volatility is a recurring structural feature of money markets, not a sign of systemic stress. The SRF serves as a targeted backstop for the uncleared tri-party segment only, while higher repo rates in other segments (particularly dealer-to-client) can persist even when the facility works as designed because operational overhead and balance sheet costs outweigh the benefit of lower SRF rates. Understanding this structural distinction is essential for interpreting repo market dynamics during periods of elevated rates.
macro_context: The Fed is conducting monetary policy in an ample reserves regime where control over short-term rates is exercised through administered rates (IORB, ON RRP) rather than active reserve management. Balance sheet runoff has been ongoing since June 2022, and Treasury net issuance continues to grow, contributing to gradual normalization of money market conditions.
market_structure_context: The speech provides detailed taxonomy of the three-segment repo market structure: tri-party (SRF-eligible, uncleared), interdealer (FICC-cleared, morning settlement), and dealer-to-client (bilateral, morning settlement, mostly hedge funds). The SRF's design constraints (afternoon settlement, no central clearing, no netting) limit its effectiveness as a backstop outside the uncleared tri-party segment. FHLB activity creates a channel linking repo dynamics to fed funds rates and volumes.
important_numbers: 21 basis points (SOFR peak above prior week average at September 2024 quarter-end)
10-25 basis points (historical range of temporary repo pressures in 2015-17)
$500 billion (overnight repo above SRF rate on one quarter-end day)
88 years (since SOMA Manager role created in 1936)
1 day (EFFR spent outside target range since January 2019 framework adoption)
